q: What historical device, constructed from mirrors and selenium detectors by Alexander Graham Bell, was the first to transmit sound waves over a beam of light?
Who coined the term 'fiber optics' in the mid-1950s?
Which range represents the standard wavelengths of visible light?
What is the primary physical phenomenon that allows light to be guided down an optical fiber without leaking through the cladding?
For total internal reflection to occur, what must be the relationship between the refractive index of the core (n1) and the cladding (n2)?
a: n1 must be greater than n2
b: n1 must be less than n2
c: n1 must be exactly equal to n2

q: What type of light ray travels down an optical fiber by continually crossing the fiber's central longitudinal axis?
What type of light ray propagates through an optical fiber in a helical or spiral path without ever intersecting the central axis?
What parameter is a measure of the light-gathering or light-collecting ability of an optical fiber?
In which type of optical fiber does the refractive index profile change abruptly at the core-cladding boundary?
In which type of optical fiber does the refractive index of the core decrease continuously as distance increases from the center axis?
What is the primary raw material used to manufacture high-purity glass optical fibers?
What standard cladding diameter is universally utilized for most telecom-grade single-mode and multimode optical fibers?
What represents the typical core diameter of a standard single-mode optical fiber?
What are the two standard core diameters for commercial multimode graded-index fibers?
What is the main advantage of graded-index fiber over step-index multimode fiber?
What is the fundamental limitation on transmission distance in single-mode fiber systems?
What is the primary cause of intermodal (or modal) dispersion in optical fibers?
a: different wavelengths of light traveling at different speeds
b: different propagation modes traveling different path lengths down the fiber
c: refraction at the outer boundary of the cladding
d: absorption by metallic impurities in the core

q: Which type of dispersion is caused by the variation of the refractive index of the glass material with wavelength?
What two sub-types of dispersion collectively make up chromatic dispersion in single-mode fibers?
At what approximate wavelength does standard single-mode silica fiber exhibit zero chromatic dispersion?
At what approximate wavelength does standard single-mode silica fiber exhibit its absolute minimum attenuation?
What non-dispersive wave pulse can propagate through an optical fiber over long distances without changing its shape?
What is the dominant loss mechanism in high-quality optical fibers at short wavelengths, caused by localized density fluctuations in the glass?
Rayleigh scattering loss is inversely proportional to which power of the wavelength?
What type of optical fiber loss is caused by microscopic, high-frequency spatial deviations or bumps along the fiber axis, often introduced during cabling?
What type of optical fiber loss occurs when a fiber cable is physically bent into a curve with a radius of several centimeters?
What is the primary cause of the prominent water-peak absorption bands observed in early optical fiber attenuation profiles?
Which optical source operates based on spontaneous emission of light?
Which optical source operates based on stimulated emission of light?
What is the typical spectral width (linewidth) of a standard surface-emitting LED?
What is the typical spectral width of a standard single-frequency Injection Laser Diode?
What semiconductor material is widely used to manufacture optical sources and detectors operating in the short-wavelength window (850 nm)?
What semiconductor compound is standard for high-performance optical sources operating in the 1310 nm and 1550 nm windows?
What semiconductor photodetector utilizes an internal avalanche multiplication process to provide high current gain and sensitivity?
What is the primary structural difference between a PIN photodiode and a standard PN junction diode?
a: An intrinsic semiconductor layer is sandwiched between the P and N regions
b: It contains an extra metal gate electrode
c: It has no depletion region under reverse bias
d: It is made of amorphous copper-doped glass

q: What is the term for the small current that flows through a photodetector even when there is no incident light striking the active area?
Which optical detector generally provides the fastest operating speed and highest bandwidth?
What is the primary advantage of a PIN photodiode over an Avalanche Photodiode (APD)?
b: Lower bias voltage requirement and lower noise
c: Greater sensitivity to extremely weak light levels

q: What does the term 'responsivity' describe in optical detectors?
a: The time required for a detector to respond to a light pulse
b: The ratio of output electrical current to input optical power
c: The percentage of incident photons that generate electron-hole pairs
d: The minimum detectable optical power level

q: What optical amplifier technology amplifies optical signals directly in the fiber without converting them back to electrical signals, operating at 1550 nm?
What pump laser wavelength is commonly used to excite erbium ions in an EDFA system?
What type of optical fiber splice permanently joins two fibers by melting their ends together using an electric arc?
What is the typical average insertion loss of a high-quality fusion splice?
What is the typical average insertion loss of a mechanical splice?
What substance is placed inside a mechanical splice or connector interface to minimize reflection losses by matching the core's refractive index?
What type of optical connection loss is caused by the physical gap or axial separation between two fiber cores?
What type of optical connection loss is caused by the physical offset or axis displacement perpendicular to the fiber axis?
What instrument is used to measure overall fiber attenuation, splice losses, and locate physical breaks by analyzing backscattered light?
What technique allows multiple optical channels of different wavelengths to be transmitted simultaneously down a single fiber?
What technology is used to multiplex a vast number of closely spaced wavelengths (typically spaced 0.8 nm or less) over the 1550 nm band?
What component is used at the receiver of a WDM system to separate the individual optical wavelengths?
What device allows light to pass in one direction with low loss, but strongly attenuates reflections traveling in the opposite direction?
What is the primary protective coating applied directly to the cladding of an optical fiber during the drawing process to prevent moisture and abrasions?
The core of an optical fiber behaves as what type of physical waveguide?
Which parameter describes the maximum angle at which a light ray can enter the core of a fiber and still undergo total internal reflection?
What three-dimensional space extending from the fiber's core face represents all possible launch angles for guided rays?
What occurs when the angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary is less than the critical angle?
a: The ray is completely reflected back into the core
b: The ray refracts into the cladding and is lost as a radiating wave
c: The ray's velocity increases above the speed of light
d: The ray's wavelength shifts to a higher value

q: Which type of optical fiber has the highest launch power capability and is typically used for short-distance power delivery?
What is the main advantage of plastic optical fiber (POF) over glass optical fiber?
b: High flexibility, ease of installation, and low cost

q: In what wavelength region do standard plastic optical fibers typically operate?
What occurs to the velocity of light as it transitions from a less dense medium (like air) into a denser medium (like glass)?
What term describes the ratio of the speed of light in a vacuum to the speed of light in a given material?
The refractive index of any optical material is always _____.
What type of optical fiber is specifically designed to shift the zero-dispersion wavelength from 1310 nm to the minimum attenuation window of 1550 nm?
What type of single-mode fiber is optimized to have a small, flat chromatic dispersion across the entire C-band (1530 to 1565 nm) to prevent non-linear effects in DWDM networks?
What physical phenomenon describes the generation of new optical frequencies when high-power channels interact in a DWDM fiber, resembling intermodulation?
What non-linear optical effect involves the scattering of light by acoustic phonons, causing a frequency shift and backward-traveling power?
What is the primary function of an optical circulator?
a: To amplify weak signals at 1550 nm
b: To route optical signals from port to port in a sequential, unidirectional path
c: To filter out unwanted noise wavelengths

q: What occurs to the V-number of an optical fiber if the operating wavelength of the light source is decreased?
What represents the theoretical limit of single-mode propagation in a step-index fiber in terms of the V-number?
Which optical detector operates based on the generation of electron-hole pairs inside a reverse-biased PN junction?
What is a major advantage of the optical receiver preamplifier known as a transimpedance amplifier (TIA)?
a: Extremely high optical input power handling
b: Excellent balance between low noise, wide dynamic range, and high bandwidth
c: Ability to regenerate optical signals without photodiodes

q: What is the term for the degradation of optical glass caused by moisture and humidity acting on micro-cracks under stress?
What represents the standard diameter of the protective buffer jacket typically applied over the cladding of a tight-buffered fiber cable?
Which fiber-optic component is used to distribute optical power from a single input fiber to multiple output fibers?
What type of optical fiber coupler is manufactured by placing two parallel fibers in contact and heating them until they fuse together?
What index of refraction represents a typical glass core used in telecom fibers?
What index of refraction represents a typical air medium?
What index of refraction represents water?
What is the physical meaning of a monochromatic light wave?
a: Light that contains all visible colors
b: Light consisting of a single, narrow frequency/wavelength
c: Light that propagates in multiple spatial modes
d: Light emitted by a spontaneous thermal source
